classes.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.nfw_phase_space.NFWPhaseSpace

methods.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.nfw_phase_space.NFWPhaseSpace.__init__
documentation:
# Parameters

conc_mass_model

: Specifies the function used to model the relation between
  NFW concentration and halo mass.
  Can either be a custom-built callable function,
  or one of the following strings:
  `dutton_maccio14`, `direct_from_halo_catalog`.

cosmology

: Instance of an astropy `~astropy.cosmology`.
  Default cosmology is set in
  `~halotools.sim_manager.sim_defaults`.

redshift

: Default is set in `~halotools.sim_manager.sim_defaults`.

mdef: str, optional

: String specifying the halo mass definition, e.g., 'vir' or '200m'.
  Default is set in `~halotools.empirical_models.model_defaults`.

halo_boundary_key

: Default behavior is to use the column associated with the input mdef.

concentration_key

: Column name of the halo catalog storing NFW concentration.

  This argument is only relevant when `conc_mass_model`
  is set to `direct_from_halo_catalog`. In such a case,
  the default value is `halo_nfw_conc`,
  which is consistent with all halo catalogs provided by Halotools
  but may differ from the convention adopted in custom halo catalogs.

concentration_bins

: Array storing how halo concentrations will be digitized when building
  a lookup table for mock-population purposes.
  The spacing of this array sets a limit on how accurately the
  concentration parameter can be recovered in a likelihood analysis.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> model = NFWPhaseSpace()
```

methods.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.nfw_phase_space.NFWPhaseSpace._retrieve_prof_lookup_info
documentation: Retrieve the arrays defining the lookup table control points

methods.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.nfw_phase_space.NFWPhaseSpace.assign_phase_space
documentation:
Primary method of the `NFWPhaseSpace` class
called during the mock-population sequence.

# Parameters

table

: `~astropy.table.Table` storing halo catalog.

  After calling the `assign_phase_space` method,
  the `x`, `y`, `z`, `vx`, `vy`, and `vz`
  columns of the input `table` will be over-written
  with their host-centric values.

seed

: Random number seed used in the Monte Carlo realization.
  Default is None, which will produce stochastic results.

methods.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.nfw_phase_space.NFWPhaseSpace.conc_NFWmodel
documentation:
NFW concentration as a function of halo mass.

# Parameters

prim_haloprop

: Array storing the mass-like variable, e.g., `halo_mvir`.

  If `prim_haloprop` is not passed,
  then `table` keyword argument must be passed.

table

: `~astropy.table.Table` storing the halo catalog.

  If your NFW model is based on the virial definition,
  then `halo_mvir` must appear in the input table,
  and likewise for other halo mass definitions.

  If `table` is not passed,
  then `prim_haloprop` keyword argument must be passed.

# Returns

conc

: Concentrations of the input halos.

  Note that concentrations will be clipped to their min/max permitted
  values set in the `~halotools.empirical_models.model_defaults` module.
  The purpose of this clipping is to ensure stable results during
  mock galaxy population. Due to this clipping,
  the behavior of the `conc_NFWmodel` function
  is different from the concentration-mass relation that underlies it.

# Examples

In the examples below, we'll demonstrate the various ways to use the
`~halotools.empirical_models.NFWPhaseSpace.conc_NFWmodel` function, depending
on the initial choice for the `conc_mass_model`.

```pycon
>>> fake_masses = np.logspace(12, 15, 10)
```

If you use the `direct_from_halo_catalog` option, you must pass a
`table` argument storing a `~astropy.table.Table` with a column name
for the halo mass that is consistent with your chosen halo mass definition:

```pycon
>>> from astropy.table import Table
>>> nfw = NFWPhaseSpace(conc_mass_model='direct_from_halo_catalog', mdef='vir')
>>> fake_conc = np.zeros_like(fake_masses) + 5.
>>> fake_halo_table = Table({'halo_mvir': fake_masses, 'halo_nfw_conc': fake_conc})
>>> model_conc = nfw.conc_NFWmodel(table=fake_halo_table)
```

In case your halo catalog uses a different keyname from the Halotools
default `halo_nfw_conc`:

```pycon
>>> nfw = NFWPhaseSpace(conc_mass_model='direct_from_halo_catalog', mdef='vir', concentration_key='my_conc_keyname')
>>> fake_halo_table = Table({'halo_mvir': fake_masses, 'my_conc_keyname': fake_conc})
>>> model_conc = nfw.conc_NFWmodel(table=fake_halo_table)
```

One of the available options provided by Halotools is `dutton_maccio14`.
With this option, you can either pass in a `table` argument, or alternatively
an array of masses via the `prim_haloprop` argument:

```pycon
>>> nfw = NFWPhaseSpace(conc_mass_model='dutton_maccio14')
>>> fake_halo_table = Table({'halo_mvir': fake_masses, 'halo_nfw_conc': fake_conc})
>>> model_conc = nfw.conc_NFWmodel(table=fake_halo_table)
>>> model_conc = nfw.conc_NFWmodel(prim_haloprop=fake_masses)
```

Finally, you may also have chosen to define your own concentration-mass relation.
If so, your function must at a minimum accept a `table` keyword argument.
Below we give a trivial example of using the identity function:

```pycon
>>> def identity_func(*args, **kwargs): return kwargs['table']['halo_mvir']
>>> nfw = NFWPhaseSpace(conc_mass_model=identity_func, mdef='vir')
>>> fake_halo_table = Table({'halo_mvir': fake_masses})
>>> model_conc = nfw.conc_NFWmodel(table=fake_halo_table)
```

methods.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.nfw_phase_space.NFWPhaseSpace.dimensionless_mass_density
documentation:
Physical density of the NFW halo scaled by the density threshold of the mass definition:

The `dimensionless_mass_density` is defined as
$\tilde{\rho}_{\rm prof}(\tilde{r}) \equiv \rho_{\rm prof}(\tilde{r}) / \rho_{\rm thresh}$,
where $\tilde{r}\equiv r/R_{\Delta}$.

For an NFW halo,
$\tilde{\rho}_{\rm NFW}(\tilde{r}, c) = \frac{c^{3}}{3g(c)}\times\frac{1}{c\tilde{r}(1 + c\tilde{r})^{2}},$

where $g(x) \equiv \int_{0}^{x}dy\frac{y}{(1+y)^{2}} = \log(1+x) - x / (1+x)$ is computed using the `g` function.

The quantity $\rho_{\rm thresh}$ is a function of
the halo mass definition, cosmology and redshift,
and is computed via the
`~halotools.empirical_models.profile_helpers.density_threshold` function.
The quantity $\rho_{\rm prof}$ is the physical mass density of the
halo profile and is computed via the `mass_density` function.

# Parameters

scaled_radius

: Halo-centric distance *r* scaled by the halo boundary $R_{\Delta}$, so that
  $0 <= \tilde{r} \equiv r/R_{\Delta} <= 1$. Can be a scalar or numpy array.

conc

: Value of the halo concentration. Can either be a scalar, or a numpy array
  of the same dimension as the input `scaled_radius`.

# Returns

dimensionless_density: array_like

: Dimensionless density of a dark matter halo
  at the input `scaled_radius`, normalized by the
  `~halotools.empirical_models.profile_helpers.density_threshold`
  $\rho_{\rm thresh}$ for the
  halo mass definition, cosmology, and redshift.
  Result is an array of the dimension as the input `scaled_radius`.

methods.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.nfw_phase_space.NFWPhaseSpace.mass_density
documentation:
Physical density of the halo at the input radius,
given in units of $h^{3}/{\rm Mpc}^{3}$.

# Parameters

radius

: Halo-centric distance in Mpc/h units; can be a scalar or numpy array

mass

: Total mass of the halo; can be a scalar or numpy array of the same
  dimension as the input `radius`.

conc

: Value of the halo concentration. Can either be a scalar, or a numpy array
  of the same dimension as the input `radius`.

# Returns

density: array_like

: Physical density of a dark matter halo of the input `mass`
  at the input `radius`. Result is an array of the
  dimension as the input `radius`, reported in units of $h^{3}/Mpc^{3}$.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> model = NFWPhaseSpace()
>>> Npts = 100
>>> radius = np.logspace(-2, -1, Npts)
>>> mass = np.zeros(Npts) + 1e12
>>> conc = 5
>>> result = model.mass_density(radius, mass, conc)
>>> concarr = np.linspace(1, 100, Npts)
>>> result = model.mass_density(radius, mass, concarr)
```

# Notes

See {ref}`halo_profile_definitions` for derivations and implementation details.

methods.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.nfw_phase_space.NFWPhaseSpace.cumulative_gal_PDF
documentation:
Analogous to `cumulative_mass_PDF`, but for the satellite galaxy distribution
instead of the host halo mass distribution.

In `~halotools.empirical_models.NFWPhaseSpace` there is no distinction between the
two methods, but in `~halotools.empirical_models.BiasedNFWPhaseSpace` these two
function are different.

# Parameters

scaled_radius

: Halo-centric distance *r* scaled by the halo boundary $R_{\Delta}$, so that
  $0 <= \tilde{r} \equiv r/R_{\Delta} <= 1$. Can be a scalar or numpy array.

conc

: Value of the halo concentration. Can either be a scalar, or a numpy array
  of the same dimension as the input `scaled_radius`.

# Returns

p: array_like

: The fraction of the total mass enclosed
  within the input `scaled_radius`, in $M_{\odot}/h$;
  has the same dimensions as the input `scaled_radius`.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> model = NFWPhaseSpace()
>>> Npts = 100
>>> scaled_radius = np.logspace(-2, 0, Npts)
>>> conc = 5
>>> result = model.cumulative_gal_PDF(scaled_radius, conc)
>>> concarr = np.linspace(1, 100, Npts)
>>> result = model.cumulative_gal_PDF(scaled_radius, concarr)
```

methods.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.nfw_phase_space.NFWPhaseSpace.cumulative_mass_PDF
documentation:
Analytical result for the fraction of the total mass
enclosed within r/Rvir of an NFW halo,

$P_{\rm NFW}(<\tilde{r}) \equiv M_{\Delta}(<\tilde{r}) / M_{\Delta} = g(c\tilde{r})/g(\tilde{r}),$

where $g(x) \equiv \int_{0}^{x}dy\frac{y}{(1+y)^{2}} = \log(1+x) - x / (1+x)$ is computed
using `g`, and where $\tilde{r} \equiv r / R_{\Delta}$.

# Parameters

scaled_radius

: Halo-centric distance *r* scaled by the halo boundary $R_{\Delta}$, so that
  $0 <= \tilde{r} \equiv r/R_{\Delta} <= 1$. Can be a scalar or numpy array.

conc

: Value of the halo concentration. Can either be a scalar, or a numpy array
  of the same dimension as the input `scaled_radius`.

# Returns

p: array_like

: The fraction of the total mass enclosed
  within the input `scaled_radius`, in $M_{\odot}/h$;
  has the same dimensions as the input `scaled_radius`.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> model = NFWPhaseSpace()
>>> Npts = 100
>>> scaled_radius = np.logspace(-2, 0, Npts)
>>> conc = 5
>>> result = model.cumulative_mass_PDF(scaled_radius, conc)
>>> concarr = np.linspace(1, 100, Npts)
>>> result = model.cumulative_mass_PDF(scaled_radius, concarr)
```

# Notes

See {ref}`halo_profile_definitions` for derivations and implementation details.

methods.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.nfw_phase_space.NFWPhaseSpace.enclosed_mass
documentation:
The mass enclosed within the input radius, $M(<r) = 4\pi\int_{0}^{r}dr'r'^{2}\rho(r)$.

# Parameters

radius

: Halo-centric distance in Mpc/h units; can be a scalar or numpy array

total_mass

: Total mass of the halo; can be a scalar or numpy array of the same
  dimension as the input `radius`.

conc

: Value of the halo concentration. Can either be a scalar, or a numpy array
  of the same dimension as the input `radius`.

# Returns

enclosed_mass: array_like

: The mass enclosed within radius r, in $M_{\odot}/h$;
  has the same dimensions as the input `radius`.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> model = NFWProfile()
>>> Npts = 100
>>> radius = np.logspace(-2, -1, Npts)
>>> total_mass = np.zeros(Npts) + 1e12
>>> conc = 5
>>> result = model.enclosed_mass(radius, total_mass, conc)
>>> concarr = np.linspace(1, 100, Npts)
>>> result = model.enclosed_mass(radius, total_mass, concarr)
```

# Notes

See {ref}`halo_profile_definitions` for derivations and implementation details.

methods.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.nfw_phase_space.NFWPhaseSpace.virial_velocity
documentation:
The circular velocity evaluated at the halo boundary,
$V_{\rm vir} \equiv \sqrt{GM_{\rm halo}/R_{\rm halo}}$.

# Parameters

total_mass

: Total mass of the halo; can be a scalar or numpy array.

# Returns

vvir

: Virial velocity in km/s.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> model = NFWProfile()
>>> Npts = 100
>>> mass_array = np.logspace(11, 15, Npts)
>>> vvir_array = model.virial_velocity(mass_array)
```

# Notes

See {ref}`halo_profile_definitions` for derivations and implementation details.

methods.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.nfw_phase_space.NFWPhaseSpace.circular_velocity
documentation:
The circular velocity, $V_{\rm cir} \equiv \sqrt{GM(<r)/r}$,
as a function of halo-centric distance r.

# Parameters

radius

: Halo-centric distance in Mpc/h units; can be a scalar or numpy array

total_mass

: Total mass of the halo; can be a scalar or numpy array of the same
  dimension as the input `radius`.

conc

: Value of the halo concentration. Can either be a scalar, or a numpy array
  of the same dimension as the input `radius`.

# Returns

vc: array_like

: The circular velocity in km/s; has the same dimensions as the input `radius`.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> model = NFWPhaseSpace()
>>> Npts = 100
>>> radius = np.logspace(-2, -1, Npts)
>>> total_mass = np.zeros(Npts) + 1e12
>>> conc = 5
>>> result = model.circular_velocity(radius, total_mass, conc)
>>> concarr = np.linspace(1, 100, Npts)
>>> result = model.circular_velocity(radius, total_mass, concarr)
```

# Notes

See {ref}`halo_profile_definitions` for derivations and implementation details.

methods.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.nfw_phase_space.NFWPhaseSpace.vmax
documentation:
Maximum circular velocity of the halo profile.

# Parameters

total_mass: array_like

: Total halo mass in $M_{\odot}/h$; can be a number or a numpy array.

conc

: Value of the halo concentration. Can either be a scalar, or a numpy array
  of the same dimension as the input `total_mass`.

# Returns

vmax

: $V_{\rm max}$ in km/s.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> model = NFWPhaseSpace()
>>> Npts = 100
>>> total_mass = np.zeros(Npts) + 1e12
>>> conc = 5
>>> result = model.vmax(total_mass, conc)
>>> concarr = np.linspace(1, 100, Npts)
>>> result = model.vmax(total_mass, concarr)
```

# Notes

See {ref}`halo_profile_definitions` for derivations and implementation details.

methods.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.nfw_phase_space.NFWPhaseSpace.halo_mass_to_halo_radius
documentation:
Spherical overdensity radius as a function of the input mass.

Note that this function is independent of the form of the density profile.

# Parameters

total_mass: array_like

: Total halo mass in $M_{\odot}/h$; can be a number or a numpy array.

# Returns

radius

: Radius of the halo in Mpc/h units.
  Will have the same dimension as the input `total_mass`.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> model = NFWPhaseSpace()
>>> halo_radius = model.halo_mass_to_halo_radius(1e13)
```

methods.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.nfw_phase_space.NFWPhaseSpace.halo_radius_to_halo_mass
documentation:
Spherical overdensity mass as a function of the input radius.

Note that this function is independent of the form of the density profile.

# Parameters

radius

: Radius of the halo in Mpc/h units; can be a number or a numpy array.

# Returns

total_mass: array_like

: Total halo mass in $M_{\odot}/h$.
  Will have the same dimension as the input `radius`.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> model = NFWPhaseSpace()
>>> halo_mass = model.halo_mass_to_halo_radius(500.)
```

methods.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.nfw_phase_space.NFWPhaseSpace.dimensionless_radial_velocity_dispersion
documentation:
Analytical solution to the isotropic jeans equation for an NFW potential,
rendered dimensionless via scaling by the virial velocity.

$\tilde{\sigma}^{2}_{r}(\tilde{r})\equiv\sigma^{2}_{r}(\tilde{r})/V_{\rm vir}^{2} = \frac{c^{2}\tilde{r}(1 + c\tilde{r})^{2}}{g(c)}\int_{c\tilde{r}}^{\infty}{\rm d}y\frac{g(y)}{y^{3}(1 + y)^{2}}$

See {ref}`nfw_jeans_velocity_profile_derivations` for derivations and implementation details.

# Parameters

scaled_radius

: Length-Ngals numpy array storing the halo-centric distance
  *r* scaled by the halo boundary $R_{\Delta}$, so that
  $0 <= \tilde{r} \equiv r/R_{\Delta} <= 1$.

conc

: Concentration of the halo.

# Returns

result

: Radial velocity dispersion profile scaled by the virial velocity.
  The returned result has the same dimension as the input `scaled_radius`.

methods.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.nfw_phase_space.NFWPhaseSpace.radial_velocity_dispersion
documentation:
Method returns the radial velocity dispersion scaled by
the virial velocity as a function of the halo-centric distance.

# Parameters

radius

: Radius of the halo in Mpc/h units; can be a float or
  ndarray of shape (num_radii, )

total_mass

: Float or ndarray of shape (num_radii, ) storing the host halo mass

halo_conc

: Float or ndarray of shape (num_radii, ) storing the host halo concentration

# Returns

result

: Radial velocity dispersion profile as a function of the input `radius`,
  in units of km/s.

methods.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.nfw_phase_space.NFWPhaseSpace.setup_prof_lookup_tables
documentation:
This method sets up how we will digitize halo concentrations during mock population.

Parameters
----------
concentration_bins : ndarray
    Array storing how concentrations will be digitized during mock-population

methods.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.nfw_phase_space.NFWPhaseSpace.build_lookup_tables
documentation:
Method used to create a lookup table of the spatial and velocity radial profiles.

Parameters
----------
logrmin : float, optional
    Minimum radius used to build the spline table.
    Default is set in `~halotools.empirical_models.model_defaults`.

logrmax : float, optional
    Maximum radius used to build the spline table
    Default is set in `~halotools.empirical_models.model_defaults`.

Npts_radius_table : int, optional
    Number of control points used in the spline.
    Default is set in `~halotools.empirical_models.model_defaults`.

methods.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.nfw_phase_space.NFWPhaseSpace.mc_unit_sphere
documentation:
Returns Npts random points on the unit sphere.

Parameters
----------
Npts : int
    Number of 3d points to generate

seed : int, optional
    Random number seed used in the Monte Carlo realization.
    Default is None, which will produce stochastic results.

Returns
-------
x, y, z : array_like
    Length-Npts arrays of the coordinate positions.

methods.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.nfw_phase_space.NFWPhaseSpace.mc_solid_sphere
documentation:
Method to generate random, three-dimensional, halo-centric positions of galaxies.

# Parameters

concentration_array

: Length-Ngals numpy array storing the concentrations of the mock galaxies.

table

: Astropy Table storing a length-Ngals galaxy catalog.
  If `table` is not passed, `concentration_array` must be passed.

seed

: Random number seed used in the Monte Carlo realization.
  Default is None, which will produce stochastic results.

# Returns

x, y, z

: Length-Ngals array storing a Monte Carlo realization of the galaxy positions.

methods.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.nfw_phase_space.NFWPhaseSpace.mc_halo_centric_pos
documentation:
Method to generate random, three-dimensional
halo-centric positions of galaxies.

# Parameters

table

: Astropy Table storing a length-Ngals galaxy catalog.
  If `table` is not passed, `concentration_array` and
  keyword argument `halo_radius` must be passed.

concentration_array

: Length-Ngals numpy array storing the concentrations of the mock galaxies.
  If `table` is not passed, `concentration_array` and
  keyword argument `halo_radius` must be passed.

halo_radius

: Length-Ngals array storing the radial boundary of the halo
  hosting each galaxy. Units assumed to be in Mpc/h.
  If `concentration_array` and `halo_radius` are not passed,
  `table` must be passed.

seed

: Random number seed used in the Monte Carlo realization.
  Default is None, which will produce stochastic results.

# Returns

x, y, z

: Length-Ngals array storing a Monte Carlo realization of the galaxy positions.

methods.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.nfw_phase_space.NFWPhaseSpace.mc_pos
documentation:
Method to generate random, three-dimensional positions of galaxies.

# Parameters

table

: Astropy Table storing a length-Ngals galaxy catalog.
  If `table` is not passed, `concentration_array` and `halo_radius` must be passed.

concentration_array

: Length-Ngals numpy array storing the concentrations of the mock galaxies.
  If `table` is not passed, `concentration_array` and
  keyword argument `halo_radius` must be passed.
  If `concentration_array` is passed, `halo_radius` must be passed as a keyword argument.
  The sequence must have the same order as `self.gal_prof_param_keys`.

halo_radius

: Length-Ngals array storing the radial boundary of the halo
  hosting each galaxy. Units assumed to be in Mpc/h.
  If `concentration_array` and `halo_radius` are not passed,
  `table` must be passed.

seed

: Random number seed used in Monte Carlo realization. Default is None.

# Returns

x, y, z

: For the case where no `table` is passed as an argument,
  method will return x, y and z points distributed about the
  origin according to the profile model.

  For the case where `table` is passed as an argument
  (this is the use case of populating halos with mock galaxies),
  the `x`, `y`, and `z` columns of the table will be over-written.
  When `table` is passed as an argument, the method
  assumes that the `x`, `y`, and `z` columns already store
  the position of the host halo center.

seed

: Random number seed used in the Monte Carlo realization.
  Default is None, which will produce stochastic results.

methods.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.nfw_phase_space.NFWPhaseSpace._vrad_disp_from_lookup
documentation:
Method to generate Monte Carlo realizations of the profile model.

# Parameters

scaled_radius

: Halo-centric distance *r* scaled by the halo boundary $R_{\Delta}$, so that
  $0 <= \tilde{r} \equiv r/R_{\Delta} <= 1$. Can be a scalar or numpy array.

concentration_array

: Length-Ngals numpy array storing the concentrations of the mock galaxies.

# Returns

sigma_vr

: Length-Ngals array containing the radial velocity dispersion
  of galaxies within their halos,
  scaled by the size of the halo's virial velocity.

methods.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.nfw_phase_space.NFWPhaseSpace.mc_radial_velocity
documentation:
Method returns a Monte Carlo realization of radial velocities drawn from Gaussians
with a width determined by the solution to the isotropic Jeans equation.

# Parameters

scaled_radius

: Halo-centric distance *r* scaled by the halo boundary $R_{\Delta}$, so that
  $0 <= \tilde{r} \equiv r/R_{\Delta} <= 1$. Can be a scalar or numpy array.

total_mass: array_like

: Length-Ngals numpy array storing the halo mass in $M_{\odot}/h$.

concentration_array

: Length-Ngals numpy array storing the concentrations of the mock galaxies.

seed

: Random number seed used in the Monte Carlo realization.
  Default is None, which will produce stochastic results.

# Returns

radial_velocities

: Array of radial velocities drawn from Gaussians with a width determined by the
  solution to the Jeans equation.

methods.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.nfw_phase_space.NFWPhaseSpace.mc_vel
documentation:
Method assigns a Monte Carlo realization of the Jeans velocity
solution to the halos in the input `table`.

# Parameters

table

: `astropy.table.Table` object storing the halo catalog.
  Calling the `mc_vel` method will over-write the existing values of
  the `vx`, `vy` and `vz` columns.

seed

: Random number seed used in the Monte Carlo realization.
  Default is None, which will produce stochastic results.

methods.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.nfw_phase_space.NFWPhaseSpace.mc_generate_nfw_phase_space_points
documentation:
Return a Monte Carlo realization of points
in the phase space of an NFW halo in isotropic Jeans equilibrium.

# Parameters

Ngals

: Number of galaxies in the Monte Carlo realization of the
  phase space distribution. Default is 1e4.

conc

: Concentration of the NFW profile being realized.
  Default is 5.

mass

: Mass of the halo whose phase space distribution is being realized
  in units of Msun/h. Default is 1e12.

verbose

: If True, a message prints with an estimate of the build time.
  Default is True.

seed

: Random number seed used in the Monte Carlo realization.
  Default is None, which will produce stochastic results.

# Returns

t

: `~astropy.table.Table` containing the Monte Carlo realization of the
  phase space distribution.
  Keys are 'x', 'y', 'z', 'vx', 'vy', 'vz', 'radial_position', 'radial_velocity'.
  Length units in Mpc/h, velocity units in km/s.

  Sign convention on the returned `radial_velocity` column is such that
  negative (positive) values correspond to
  satellites moving radially inward (outward)

# Examples

```pycon
>>> nfw = NFWPhaseSpace()
>>> mass, conc = 1e13, 8.
>>> data = nfw.mc_generate_nfw_phase_space_points(Ngals=100, mass=mass, conc=conc, verbose=False)
```

Now suppose you wish to compute the radial velocity dispersion of all the returned points:

```pycon
>>> vrad_disp = np.std(data['radial_velocity'])
```

If you wish to do the same calculation but for points in a specific range of radius:

```pycon
>>> mask = data['radial_position'] < 0.1
>>> vrad_disp_inner_points = np.std(data['radial_velocity'][mask])
```

You may also wish to select points according to their distance to the halo center
in units of the virial radius. In such as case, you can use the
`~halotools.empirical_models.NFWPhaseSpace.halo_mass_to_halo_radius`
method to scale the halo-centric distances. Here is an example
of how to compute the velocity dispersion in the z-dimension of all points
residing within $R_{\rm vir}/2$:

```pycon
>>> halo_radius = nfw.halo_mass_to_halo_radius(mass)
>>> scaled_radial_positions = data['radial_position']/halo_radius
>>> mask = scaled_radial_positions < 0.5
>>> vz_disp_inner_half = np.std(data['vz'][mask])
```

Model for the phase space distribution of mass and/or galaxies
in isotropic Jeans equilibrium in an NFW halo profile,
based on Navarro, Frenk and White (1995),
where the concentration of the galaxies is the same
as the concentration of the parent halo

For a review of the mathematics underlying the NFW profile,
including descriptions of how the relevant equations are
implemented in the Halotools code base, see {ref}`nfw_profile_tutorial`.

modules.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.tests.test_nfw_profile.test_nfw_profile
documentation: Module providing unit-testing for `~halotools.empirical_models.NFWProfile` class

functions.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.tests.test_nfw_profile.test_nfw_profile.test_attr_inheritance
documentation:
Test that `~halotools.empirical_models.NFWProfile`
possesses the necessary attributes and methods.

functions.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.tests.test_nfw_profile.test_nfw_profile.analytic_nfw_density_outer_shell_normalization
documentation:
Density of an NFW profile normalized by the density evaluated at the outermost value of the input `radii` array.

For an NFW profile we have the following analytical relation:

$\rho(r_{2})/\rho(r_{1}) = \frac{r_{1}(1+cr_{1})^{2}}{r_{2}(1+cr_{2})^{2}}$.

The `TestNFWProfile.test_mc_generate_nfw_radial_positions` test demonstrates that Monte Carlo realizations
of `~halotools.empirical_models.NFWProfile` generated by the
`~halotools.empirical_models.NFWProfile.mc_generate_nfw_radial_positions`
method respect this relation.

# Parameters

radii

: Array of halo-centric distances in an NFW profile.

conc

: Concentration of the halo for which this relation is being tested.

# Returns

result

: Ratio of the NFW density scaled by the density value at the outermost value of the input `radii`.

functions.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.tests.test_nfw_profile.test_nfw_profile.monte_carlo_density_outer_shell_normalization
documentation:
Density of a Monte Carlo realization of a spherically symmetric profile normalized by the density evaluated at the midpoint of the outermost bin of the input `rbins` array.

# Parameters

rbins

: Array defining how the input `radial_positions` will be binned.

radial_positions

: Array storing the Monte Carlo realization of the profile.

# Returns

rbin_midpoints

: Midpoints of the input `rbins`.

result

: Ratio of the profile number density scaled by the number density at the midpoint of the outermost bin of the input `rbins` array.

functions.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.tests.test_nfw_profile.test_nfw_profile.test_instance_attrs
documentation: Require that all model variants have `cosmology`, `redshift` and `mdef` attributes.

functions.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.tests.test_nfw_profile.test_nfw_profile.test_mass_density
documentation:
Require the returned value of the
`~halotools.empirical_models.NFWProfile.mass_density` function
to be self-consistent with the returned value of the
`~halotools.empirical_models.NFWProfile.dimensionless_mass_density` function.

functions.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.tests.test_nfw_profile.test_nfw_profile.test_cumulative_mass_PDF
documentation:
Require the `~halotools.empirical_models.NFWProfile.cumulative_mass_PDF`
method in all model variants to respect a number of consistency conditions.

1. Returned value is a strictly monotonically increasing array between 0 and 1.
2. Returned value is consistent with the following expression,

$P_{\rm NFW}(<\tilde{r}) = 4\pi\int_{0}^{\tilde{r}}d\tilde{r}\tilde{r}'^{2}\tilde{\rho}_{NFW}(\tilde{r}),$

In the test suite implementation of the above equation,
the LHS is computed by the analytical expression given in
`~halotools.empirical_models.NFWProfile.cumulative_mass_PDF`,
$P_{\rm NFW}(<\tilde{r}) = g(c\tilde{r})/g(\tilde{r})$, where the function
$g(x) \equiv \int_{0}^{x}dy\frac{y}{(1+y)^{2}} = \log(1+x) - x / (1+x)$
is computed using the
`~halotools.empirical_models.NFWProfile.g` method of the
`~halotools.empirical_models.NFWProfile` class.

The RHS of the consistency equation is computed by performing a direct numerical integral of

$\tilde{\rho}_{\rm NFW}(\tilde{r}) \equiv \rho_{\rm NFW}(\tilde{r})/\rho_{\rm thresh} = \frac{c^{3}}{3g(c)}\times\frac{1}{c\tilde{r}(1 + c\tilde{r})^{2}}.$
where in the test suite implementation $\tilde{\rho}_{\rm NFW}(\tilde{r})$ is computed
using the `~halotools.empirical_models.NFWProfile.dimensionless_mass_density`
method of the `~halotools.empirical_models.NFWProfile` class.

3. $M_{\Delta}(<r) = M_{\Delta}\times P_{\rm NFW}(<r)$.

functions.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.tests.test_nfw_profile.test_nfw_profile.test_vmax
documentation:
Require that the analytic approximation used to estimate the NFW $V_{\rm max}$
by the `~halotools.empirical_models.NFWProfile.vmax` method
agrees with the maximum value of $V_{\rm circ}(r)$ computed over the entire profile
of the halo computed using the super-class method
`~halotools.empirical_models.profile_model_template.AnalyticDensityProf.dimensionless_circular_velocity` method.

functions.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.tests.test_nfw_profile.test_nfw_profile.test_mc_generate_nfw_radial_positions
documentation:
Require that the points returned by the
`~halotools.empirical_models.NFWProfile.mc_generate_nfw_radial_positions`
function do indeed trace an NFW profile.

The basic idea is as follows. By the method of transformation of random variates,
we can generate a Monte Carlo realization of an NFW profile provided that we have the mapping
$P^{-1}_{\rm NFW}(<\tilde{r})$. The method
`~halotools.empirical_models.NFWProfile.mc_generate_nfw_radial_positions`
obtains this mapping by first computing $P_{\rm NFW}(<\tilde{r})$ at a set of abscissa,
and then using `~scipy.interpolate.InterpolatedUnivariateSpline` to tabulate the inverse mapping.
In the Halotools implementation, the code calling the inverse mapping is
`~halotools.empirical_models.custom_spline`, which is just a convenience wrapper
around `~scipy.interpolate.InterpolatedUnivariateSpline` that handles edge cases a bit more intuitively.

For volume elements evenly spaced in $\tilde{r}$, any NFW profile must respect the following relation:

$\rho(r_{2})/\rho(r_{1}) = \frac{r_{1}(1+cr_{1})^{2}}{r_{2}(1+cr_{2})^{2}}$,

The `test_mc_generate_nfw_radial_positions` function enforces that this relation holds using
$\tilde{r}_{1} \approx 1$.
After generating a set of radial positions, the
`analytic_nfw_density_outer_shell_normalization` returns the analytical result for
$\rho(r_{2})/\rho(r_{1})$ for the given `rbins`, and the
`monte_carlo_density_outer_shell_normalization` method computes this ratio for the
Monte Carlo realization. As shown in the source code, these two quantities agree with each other
to better than 5% when using 20 bins linearly-spaced between 0.05 and 1 and
2e5 Monte Carlo-generated points, for NFW concentrations of 5, 10, and 25.

functions.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.tests.test_nfw_profile.test_nfw_profile.test_user_defined_halo_radius
documentation: Regression test for #940

modules.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.sfr_biased_nfw_phase_space
documentation:
Module defining the `~halotools.empirical_models.SFRBiasedNFWPhaseSpace` class
governing the phase space distribution of massless tracers of an NFW potential,
where the concentration of the tracers is permitted to differ from the
host halo concentration.

classes.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.sfr_biased_nfw_phase_space.SFRBiasedNFWPhaseSpace

methods.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.sfr_biased_nfw_phase_space.SFRBiasedNFWPhaseSpace.__init__
documentation:
# Parameters

conc_gal_bias_logM_abscissa

: Numpy array of shape (num_gal_bias_bins, ) storing the values of
  log10(Mhalo). For each entry of `conc_gal_bias_logM_abscissa`,
  there will be a corresponding parameter in the `param_dict` allowing
  you to vary the strength of the galaxy concentration bias,
  using log-linear interpolation for the intermediate values
  between these control points.

conc_mass_model

: Specifies the function used to model the relation between
  NFW concentration and halo mass.
  Can either be a custom-built callable function,
  or one of the following strings:
  `dutton_maccio14`, `direct_from_halo_catalog`.

cosmology

: Instance of an astropy `~astropy.cosmology`.
  Default cosmology is set in
  `~halotools.sim_manager.sim_defaults`.

redshift

: Default is set in `~halotools.sim_manager.sim_defaults`.

mdef: str, optional

: String specifying the halo mass definition, e.g., 'vir' or '200m'.
  Default is set in `~halotools.empirical_models.model_defaults`.

concentration_key

: Column name of the halo catalog storing NFW concentration.

  This argument is only relevant when `conc_mass_model`
  is set to `direct_from_halo_catalog`. In such a case,
  the default value is `halo_nfw_conc`,
  which is consistent with all halo catalogs provided by Halotools
  but may differ from the convention adopted in custom halo catalogs.

concentration_bins

: Array storing how halo concentrations will be digitized when building
  a lookup table for mock-population purposes.
  The spacing of this array sets a limit on how accurately the
  concentration parameter can be recovered in a likelihood analysis.

conc_gal_bias_bins

: Array storing how biases in galaxy concentrations will be digitized
  when building a lookup table for mock-population purposes.
  The spacing of this array sets a limit on how accurately the
  galaxy bias parameter can be recovered in a likelihood analysis.

profile_integration_tol

: Default is 1e-5

# Examples

```pycon
>>> biased_nfw = SFRBiasedNFWPhaseSpace()
```

The behavior of the `~halotools.empirical_models.SFRBiasedNFWPhaseSpace`
model is controlled by the values
stored in its `param_dict`. In the above default model, we have two parameters
that control the concentration of the satellites,
`quiescent_conc_gal_bias` and `star_forming_conc_gal_bias`.
The value of $F_{\rm q}$ = `quiescent_conc_gal_bias` sets the value for
the concentration of quiescent satellites according to
$F_{\rm q} * c_{\rm halo},$ where $c_{\rm halo}$ is the
value of the halo concentration specified by the concentration-mass model.

By default, $F_{\rm q} = F_{\rm sf} = 1$.
Quiescent satellites with *larger* values of
$F_{\rm q}$ will be *more* radially concentrated and have *smaller*
radial velocity dispersions, and likewise for star-forming galaxies.

The `SFRBiasedNFWPhaseSpace` gives you the option to allow $F_{\rm q}$
and $F_{\rm sf}$ to vary with halo mass. You can accomplish this via the
`conc_gal_bias_logM_abscissa` keyword:

```pycon
>>> biased_nfw_mass_dep = SFRBiasedNFWPhaseSpace(conc_gal_bias_logM_abscissa=[12., 15.])
```

In the above model, the values of $F_{\rm q} = F_{\rm q}(M_{\rm halo})$
and $F_{\rm sf} = F_{\rm sf}(M_{\rm halo})$
can be independently specified at either of the two control points,
$10^{12}M_{\odot}$ or $10^{15}M_{\odot}$.
For every element in the input `conc_gal_bias_logM_abscissa`, there is a
correposponding value in the `param_dict` controlling the value of
$F_{\rm q}$ and $F_{\rm sf}$ at that mass.

For example, in the `biased_nfw_mass_dep` defined above,
to set the value of $F_{\rm q}(M_{\rm halo} = 10^{15})$:

```pycon
>>> biased_nfw_mass_dep.param_dict['quiescent_conc_gal_bias_param1'] = 2
```

To triple the value of $F_{\rm sf}(M_{\rm halo} = 10^{12})$:

```pycon
>>> biased_nfw_mass_dep.param_dict['star_forming_conc_gal_bias_param0'] *= 3
```

Values of $F_{\rm q}$ and $F_{\rm sf}$ at masses
between the control points will be determined by log-linear interpolation.
When extrapolating $F_{\rm q}$ and $F_{\rm sf}$ beyond
the specified range, the values will be kept constant at the end point values.

methods.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.sfr_biased_nfw_phase_space.SFRBiasedNFWPhaseSpace._initialize_conc_bias_param_dict
documentation:
Set up the appropriate number of keys in the parameter dictionary
and give the keys standardized names.

methods.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.sfr_biased_nfw_phase_space.SFRBiasedNFWPhaseSpace.calculate_conc_gal_bias
documentation:
Calculate the ratio of the galaxy concentration to the halo concentration,
$c_{\rm gal}/c_{\rm halo}$.

# Parameters

prim_haloprop

: Array storing the mass-like variable, e.g., `halo_mvir`.

  If `prim_haloprop` is not passed,
  then `table` keyword argument must be passed.

quiescent

: Boolean array storing whether the galaxy is quiescent.
  Must be passed together with prim_haloprop argument.

table

: `~astropy.table.Table` storing the halo catalog.

  If your NFW model is based on the virial definition,
  then `halo_mvir` must appear in the input table,
  and likewise for other halo mass definitions.

  If `table` is not passed,
  then `prim_haloprop` and `quiescent` keyword arguments must be passed.

# Returns

conc_gal_bias

: Ratio of the galaxy concentration to the halo concentration,
  $F_{\rm gal} = c_{\rm gal}/c_{\rm halo}$.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> model = SFRBiasedNFWPhaseSpace()
>>> mass = np.logspace(10, 15, 100)
>>> quiescent = np.zeros(100).astype(bool)
>>> quiescent[::2] = True
>>> conc_gal_bias = model.calculate_conc_gal_bias(prim_haloprop=mass, quiescent=quiescent)
```

Model for the phase space distribution of galaxies
in isotropic Jeans equilibrium in an NFW halo profile,
based on Navarro, Frenk and White (1995),
where the concentration of the tracers is permitted to differ from the
host halo concentration, independently for red and blue galaxies.

For a review of the mathematics underlying the NFW profile,
including descriptions of how the relevant equations are
implemented in the Halotools code base, see {ref}`nfw_profile_tutorial`.

# Notes

The `SFRBiasedNFWPhaseSpace` class can only be used to make mocks in concert
with some other component model that is responsible for modeling an
`quiescent` property of the `galaxy_table`.

methods.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.biased_nfw_phase_space.BiasedNFWPhaseSpace.__init__
documentation:
# Parameters

conc_gal_bias_logM_abscissa

: Numpy array of shape (num_gal_bias_bins, ) storing the values of
  log10(Mhalo). For each entry of `conc_gal_bias_logM_abscissa`,
  there will be a corresponding parameter in the `param_dict` allowing
  you to vary the strength of the galaxy concentration bias,
  using log-linear interpolation for the intermediate values
  between these control points.

conc_mass_model

: Specifies the function used to model the relation between
  NFW concentration and halo mass.
  Can either be a custom-built callable function,
  or one of the following strings:
  `dutton_maccio14`, `direct_from_halo_catalog`.

cosmology

: Instance of an astropy `~astropy.cosmology`.
  Default cosmology is set in
  `~halotools.sim_manager.sim_defaults`.

redshift

: Default is set in `~halotools.sim_manager.sim_defaults`.

mdef: str, optional

: String specifying the halo mass definition, e.g., 'vir' or '200m'.
  Default is set in `~halotools.empirical_models.model_defaults`.

halo_boundary_key

: Default behavior is to use the column associated with the input mdef.

concentration_key

: Column name of the halo catalog storing NFW concentration.

  This argument is only relevant when `conc_mass_model`
  is set to `direct_from_halo_catalog`. In such a case,
  the default value is `halo_nfw_conc`,
  which is consistent with all halo catalogs provided by Halotools
  but may differ from the convention adopted in custom halo catalogs.

concentration_bins

: Array storing how halo concentrations will be digitized when building
  a lookup table for mock-population purposes.
  The spacing of this array sets a limit on how accurately the
  concentration parameter can be recovered in a likelihood analysis.

conc_gal_bias_bins

: Array storing how biases in galaxy concentrations will be digitized
  when building a lookup table for mock-population purposes.
  The spacing of this array sets a limit on how accurately the
  galaxy bias parameter can be recovered in a likelihood analysis.

profile_integration_tol

: Default is 1e-5

# Examples

```pycon
>>> biased_nfw = BiasedNFWPhaseSpace()
```

The behavior of the `~halotools.empirical_models.BiasedNFWPhaseSpace`
model is controlled by the values
stored in its `param_dict`. In the above default model, we have one parameter
that controls the concentration of the satellites,
`conc_gal_bias`.
The value of $F_{\rm gal}$ = `conc_gal_bias` sets the value for
the concentration of satellites according to
$F_{\rm gal} * c_{\rm halo},$ where $c_{\rm halo}$ is the
value of the halo concentration specified by the concentration-mass model.

By default, $F_{\rm gal} = 1$. Satellites with *larger* values of
$F_{\rm gal}$ will be *more* radially concentrated and have *smaller*
radial velocity dispersions.

The `BiasedNFWPhaseSpace` gives you the option to allow $F_{\rm gal}$
to vary with halo mass. You can accomplish this via the
`conc_gal_bias_logM_abscissa` keyword:

```pycon
>>> biased_nfw_mass_dep = BiasedNFWPhaseSpace(conc_gal_bias_logM_abscissa=[12., 15.])
```

In the above model, the values of $F_{\rm gal} = F_{\rm gal}(M_{\rm halo})$
can be independently specified at either of the two control points,
$10^{12}M_{\odot}$ or $10^{15}M_{\odot}$.
For every element in the input `conc_gal_bias_logM_abscissa`, there is a
correposponding value in the `param_dict` controlling the value of
$F_{\rm gal}$ at that mass.

For example, in the `biased_nfw_mass_dep` defined above,
to set the value of $F_{\rm gal}(M_{\rm halo} = 10^{15})$:

```pycon
>>> biased_nfw_mass_dep.param_dict['conc_gal_bias_param1'] = 2
```

To triple the value of $F_{\rm gal}(M_{\rm halo} = 10^{12})$:

```pycon
>>> biased_nfw_mass_dep.param_dict['conc_gal_bias_param0'] *= 3
```

Values of $F_{\rm gal}$ at masses
between the control points will be determined by log-linear interpolation.
When extrapolating $F_{\rm gal}$ beyond
the specified range, the values will be kept constant at the end point values.

methods.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.biased_nfw_phase_space.BiasedNFWPhaseSpace.conc_NFWmodel
documentation:
NFW concentration as a function of halo mass.

# Parameters

prim_haloprop

: Array storing the mass-like variable, e.g., `halo_mvir`.

  If `prim_haloprop` is not passed,
  then `table` keyword argument must be passed.

table

: `~astropy.table.Table` storing the halo catalog.

  If your NFW model is based on the virial definition,
  then `halo_mvir` must appear in the input table,
  and likewise for other halo mass definitions.

  If `table` is not passed,
  then `prim_haloprop` keyword argument must be passed.

# Returns

halo_conc

: Concentrations of the halos.

  Note that concentrations will be clipped to their min/max permitted
  values set in the `~halotools.empirical_models.model_defaults module`.
  For unclipped concentrations, set these variables to -np.inf, +np.inf.

methods.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.biased_nfw_phase_space.BiasedNFWPhaseSpace._clipped_galaxy_concentration

Private method used to ensure that biased concentrations are still
bound by the min/max bounds permitted by the lookup tables.

methods.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.biased_nfw_phase_space.BiasedNFWPhaseSpace.cumulative_gal_PDF
documentation:
Analogous to `cumulative_mass_PDF`, but for the satellite galaxy distribution
instead of the host halo mass distribution.

# Parameters

scaled_radius

: Halo-centric distance *r* scaled by the halo boundary $R_{\Delta}$, so that
  $0 <= \tilde{r} \equiv r/R_{\Delta} <= 1$. Can be a scalar or numpy array.

halo_conc

: Value of the halo concentration. Can either be a scalar, or a numpy array
  of the same dimension as the input `scaled_radius`.

conc_gal_bias

: Ratio of the galaxy concentration to the halo concentration,
  $c_{\rm gal}/c_{\rm halo}$.

# Returns

p: array_like

: The fraction of the total mass enclosed
  within the input `scaled_radius`, in $M_{\odot}/h$;
  has the same dimensions as the input `scaled_radius`.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> model = BiasedNFWPhaseSpace()
```

```pycon
>>> scaled_radius = 0.5  # units of Rvir
>>> halo_conc = 5
>>> conc_gal_bias = 3
>>> result1 = model.cumulative_gal_PDF(scaled_radius, halo_conc, conc_gal_bias)
```

```pycon
>>> num_halos = 50
>>> scaled_radius = np.logspace(-2, 0, num_halos)
>>> halo_conc = np.linspace(1, 25, num_halos)
>>> conc_gal_bias = np.zeros(num_halos) + 2.
>>> result2 = model.cumulative_gal_PDF(scaled_radius, halo_conc, conc_gal_bias)
```

methods.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.biased_nfw_phase_space.BiasedNFWPhaseSpace.cumulative_mass_PDF
documentation:
Analytical result for the fraction of the total mass
enclosed within r/Rvir of an NFW halo,

$P_{\rm NFW}(<\tilde{r}) \equiv M_{\Delta}(<\tilde{r}) / M_{\Delta} = g(c\tilde{r})/g(\tilde{r}),$

where $g(x) \equiv \int_{0}^{x}dy\frac{y}{(1+y)^{2}} = \log(1+x) - x / (1+x)$ is computed
using `g`, and where $\tilde{r} \equiv r / R_{\Delta}$.

# Parameters

scaled_radius

: Halo-centric distance *r* scaled by the halo boundary $R_{\Delta}$, so that
  $0 <= \tilde{r} \equiv r/R_{\Delta} <= 1$. Can be a scalar or numpy array.

halo_conc

: Value of the halo concentration. Can either be a scalar, or a numpy array
  of the same dimension as the input `scaled_radius`.

# Returns

p: array_like

: The fraction of the total mass enclosed
  within the input `scaled_radius`, in $M_{\odot}/h$;
  has the same dimensions as the input `scaled_radius`.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> model = NFWPhaseSpace()
>>> Npts = 100
>>> scaled_radius = np.logspace(-2, 0, Npts)
>>> halo_conc = 5
>>> result = model.cumulative_mass_PDF(scaled_radius, halo_conc)
>>> halo_conc_arr = np.linspace(1, 100, Npts)
>>> result = model.cumulative_mass_PDF(scaled_radius, halo_conc_arr)
```

# Notes

See {ref}`halo_profile_definitions` for derivations and implementation details.

methods.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.biased_nfw_phase_space.BiasedNFWPhaseSpace.dimensionless_radial_velocity_dispersion
documentation:
Analytical solution to the isotropic jeans equation for an NFW potential,
rendered dimensionless via scaling by the virial velocity.

$\tilde{\sigma}^{2}_{r}(\tilde{r})\equiv\sigma^{2}_{r}(\tilde{r})/V_{\rm vir}^{2} = \frac{c^{2}\tilde{r}(1 + c\tilde{r})^{2}}{g(c)}\int_{c\tilde{r}}^{\infty}{\rm d}y\frac{g(y)}{y^{3}(1 + y)^{2}}$

See {ref}`nfw_jeans_velocity_profile_derivations` for derivations and implementation details.

# Parameters

scaled_radius

: Length-Ngals numpy array storing the halo-centric distance
  *r* scaled by the halo boundary $R_{\Delta}$, so that
  $0 <= \tilde{r} \equiv r/R_{\Delta} <= 1$.

halo_conc

: Concentration of the halo.

# Returns

result

: Radial velocity dispersion profile scaled by the virial velocity.
  The returned result has the same dimension as the input `scaled_radius`.

methods.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.biased_nfw_phase_space.BiasedNFWPhaseSpace.radial_velocity_dispersion
documentation:
Method returns the radial velocity dispersion scaled by
the virial velocity as a function of the halo-centric distance.

# Parameters

radius

: Radius of the halo in Mpc/h units; can be a float or
  ndarray of shape (num_radii, )

total_mass

: Float or ndarray of shape (num_radii, ) storing the host halo mass

halo_conc

: Float or ndarray of shape (num_radii, ) storing the host halo concentration

conc_gal_bias

: Ratio of the galaxy concentration to the halo concentration,
  $c_{\rm gal}/c_{\rm halo}$.

# Returns

result

: Radial velocity dispersion profile as a function of the input `radius`,
  in units of km/s.

documentation:

# Parameters

prim_haloprop

  If `prim_haloprop` is not passed,

  If your NFW model is based on the virial definition,

  and likewise for other halo mass definitions.

  If `table` is not passed,
  then `prim_haloprop` keyword argument must be passed.

# Returns

conc_gal_bias

: Ratio of the galaxy concentration to the halo concentration,
  $c_{\rm gal}/c_{\rm halo}$.

Model for the phase space distribution of galaxies
in isotropic Jeans equilibrium in an NFW halo profile,
based on Navarro, Frenk and White (1995),
where the concentration of the tracers is permitted to differ from the
host halo concentration.

For a review of the mathematics underlying the NFW profile,
including descriptions of how the relevant equations are

functions.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.kernels.mc_generate_nfw_radial_positions.mc_generate_nfw_radial_positions
documentation:
Return a Monte Carlo realization of points in an NFW profile.

See {ref}`monte_carlo_nfw_spatial_profile` for a discussion of this technique.

# Parameters

num_pts

: Number of points in the Monte Carlo realization of the profile.
  Default is 1e4.

conc

: Concentration of the NFW profile being realized.
  Default is 5.

halo_mass

: Total mass of the halo whose profile is being realized.

  If `halo_mass` is unspecified,
  keyword argument `halo_radius` must be specified.

halo_radius

: Physical boundary of the halo whose profile is being realized
  in units of Mpc/h.

  If `halo_radius` is unspecified,
  keyword argument `halo_mass` must be specified, in which case the
  outer boundary of the halo will be determined
  according to the selected mass definition

cosmology

: Instance of an Astropy `~astropy.cosmology` object.
  Default is set in `~halotools.sim_manager.sim_defaults`

redshift: array_like, optional

: Can either be a scalar, or a numpy array of the same dimension as the input `halo_mass`.
  Default is set in `~halotools.sim_manager.sim_defaults`

mdef: str

: String specifying the halo mass definition, e.g., 'vir' or '200m'.
  Default is set in `~halotools.empirical_models.model_defaults`

seed

: Random number seed used in the Monte Carlo realization.
  Default is None, which will produce stochastic results.

# Returns

radial_positions

: Numpy array storing a Monte Carlo realization of the halo profile.
  All values will lie strictly between 0 and the halo boundary.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> radial_positions = mc_generate_nfw_radial_positions(halo_mass = 1e12, conc = 10)
>>> radial_positions = mc_generate_nfw_radial_positions(halo_radius = 0.25)
```

functions.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.kernels.unbiased_isotropic_velocity.dimensionless_radial_velocity_dispersion
documentation:
Analytical solution to the isotropic jeans equation for an NFW potential,
rendered dimensionless via scaling by the virial velocity.

$\tilde{\sigma}^{2}_{r}(\tilde{r})\equiv\sigma^{2}_{r}(\tilde{r})/V_{\rm vir}^{2} = \frac{c^{2}\tilde{r}(1 + c\tilde{r})^{2}}{g(c)}\int_{c\tilde{r}}^{\infty}{\rm d}y\frac{g(y)}{y^{3}(1 + y)^{2}}$

See {ref}`nfw_jeans_velocity_profile_derivations` for derivations and implementation details.

# Parameters

scaled_radius

: Length-Ngals numpy array storing the halo-centric distance
  *r* scaled by the halo boundary $R_{\Delta}$, so that
  $0 <= \tilde{r} \equiv r/R_{\Delta} <= 1$.

conc

: Concentration of the halo.

# Returns

result

: Radial velocity dispersion profile scaled by the virial velocity.
  The returned result has the same dimension as the input `scaled_radius`.

See {ref}`nfw_jeans_velocity_profile_derivations` for derivations and implementation details.

halo_conc

Convenience function used to evaluate the profile.

> $g(x) \equiv \rm{ln}(1+x) - x / (1+x)$

# Parameters

x : array_like

# Returns

```pycon
>>> result = _g_integral(1)
>>> Npts = 25
>>> result = _g_integral(np.logspace(-1, 1, Npts))
```

functions.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.kernels.mass_profile.cumulative_mass_PDF
documentation:
Analytical result for the fraction of the total mass
enclosed within dimensionless radius of an NFW halo,

$P_{\rm NFW}(<\tilde{r}) \equiv M_{\Delta}(<\tilde{r}) / M_{\Delta} = g(c\tilde{r})/g(\tilde{r}),$

where $g(x) \equiv \int_{0}^{x}dy\frac{y}{(1+y)^{2}} = \log(1+x) - x / (1+x)$ is computed
using `g`, and where $\tilde{r} \equiv r / R_{\Delta}$.

See {ref}`nfw_cumulative_mass_pdf_derivation` for a derivation of this expression.

# Parameters

scaled_radius

: Halo-centric distance *r* scaled by the halo boundary $R_{\Delta}$, so that
  $0 <= \tilde{r} \equiv r/R_{\Delta} <= 1$. Can be a scalar or numpy array.

conc

: Value of the halo concentration. Can either be a scalar, or a numpy array
  of the same dimension as the input `scaled_radius`.

# Returns

p: array_like

: The fraction of the total mass enclosed
  within the input `scaled_radius`, in $M_{\odot}/h$;
  has the same dimensions as the input `scaled_radius`.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Npts = 100
>>> scaled_radius = np.logspace(-2, 0, Npts)
>>> conc = 5
>>> result = cumulative_mass_PDF(scaled_radius, conc)
>>> concarr = np.linspace(1, 100, Npts)
>>> result = cumulative_mass_PDF(scaled_radius, concarr)
```

functions.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.kernels.mass_profile.dimensionless_mass_density
documentation:
Physical density of the NFW halo scaled by the density threshold of the mass definition.

The `dimensionless_mass_density` is defined as
$\tilde{\rho}_{\rm prof}(\tilde{r}) \equiv \rho_{\rm prof}(\tilde{r}) / \rho_{\rm thresh}$,
where $\tilde{r}\equiv r/R_{\Delta}$.

For an NFW halo,
$\tilde{\rho}_{\rm NFW}(\tilde{r}, c) = \frac{c^{3}/3g(c)}{c\tilde{r}(1 + c\tilde{r})^{2}},$

where $g(x) \equiv \log(1+x) - x / (1+x)$ is computed using the `g` function.

The quantity $\rho_{\rm thresh}$ is a function of
the halo mass definition, cosmology and redshift,
and is computed via the
`~halotools.empirical_models.profile_helpers.density_threshold` function.
The quantity $\rho_{\rm prof}$ is the physical mass density of the
halo profile and is computed via the `mass_density` function.
See {ref}`nfw_spatial_profile_derivations` for a derivation of this expression.

# Parameters

scaled_radius

: Halo-centric distance *r* scaled by the halo boundary $R_{\Delta}$, so that
  $0 <= \tilde{r} \equiv r/R_{\Delta} <= 1$. Can be a scalar or numpy array.

conc

: Value of the halo concentration. Can either be a scalar, or a numpy array
  of the same dimension as the input `scaled_radius`.

# Returns

dimensionless_density: array_like

: Dimensionless density of a dark matter halo
  at the input `scaled_radius`, normalized by the
  `~halotools.empirical_models.profile_helpers.density_threshold`
  $\rho_{\rm thresh}$ for the
  halo mass definition, cosmology, and redshift.
  Result is an array of the dimension as the input `scaled_radius`.

functions.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.conc_mass.dutton_maccio14.dutton_maccio14
documentation:
Power-law fit to the concentration-mass relation from
Equations 12 & 13 of Dutton and Maccio 2014, arXiv:1402.7073.

$\log_{10}c(M, z) \equiv a + b\log_{10}(M / M_{0}),$

where $a, b, M_{0}$ are defined as follows:

$a = 0.537 + (1.025 - 0.537)\exp(-0.718z^{1.08})$

$b = -0.097 + 0.024z$

$M_{0} = 10^{12}M_{\odot}/h$

# Parameters

mass : array_like

redshift : array_like

# Returns

concentration : array_like

# Notes

This model is based on virial mass definition and
was only calibrated for the Planck 1-year cosmology.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> c = dutton_maccio14(1e12, 0)
>>> c = dutton_maccio14(np.logspace(11, 15, 100), 0)
```

modules.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.centrals.trivial_phase_space
documentation:
This module contains the `~halotools.empirical_models.TrivialPhaseSpace` class
used to place central galaxies at the center of, and at rest with respect to, their host halo.

host halo with the exact same velocity as the halo velocity.

$P(\vec{x}_{\rm cen}, \vec{v}_{\rm cen}) = \delta^{(6)}(\vec{x}_{\rm halo}, \vec{v}_{\rm halo})$.

Physical density of the halo scaled by the density threshold of the mass definition.

The `dimensionless_mass_density` is defined as
$\tilde{\rho}_{\rm prof}(\tilde{r}) \equiv \rho_{\rm prof}(\tilde{r}) / \rho_{\rm thresh}$,
where $\tilde{r}\equiv r/R_{\Delta}$.

# Parameters

scaled_radius

: Total halo mass in $M_{\odot}/h$; can be a number or a numpy array.

: Dimensionless density of a dark matter halo

methods.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.centrals.trivial_profile.TrivialProfile.enclosed_mass
documentation:
The mass enclosed within the input radius, $M(<r) = 4\pi\int_{0}^{r}dr'r'^{2}\rho(r)$.

For the `TrivialProfile`, this is equal to the total mass of the halo for all non-zero radii.

# Parameters

radius

: Halo-centric distance in Mpc/h units; can be a scalar or numpy array

total_mass

  dimension as the input `radius`.

enclosed_mass: array_like

: The mass enclosed within radius r, in $M_{\odot}/h$;

Profile of dark matter halos with
all their mass concentrated at exactly the halo center.

modules.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.nfw_profile
documentation:
This module contains the `NFWProfile` class,
which is used to model the spatial distribution of mass and/or galaxies
inside dark matter halos according to the fitting function introduced in
Navarry, Frenk and White (1995), `arXiv:9508025 <http://arxiv.org/abs/astro-ph/9508025/>`_.
a sub-class of `~halotools.empirical_models.AnalyticDensityProf`.

classes.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.nfw_profile.NFWProfile

methods.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.nfw_profile.NFWProfile.__init__
documentation:
# Parameters

cosmology

: Instance of an astropy `~astropy.cosmology`.
  Default cosmology is set in
  `~halotools.sim_manager.sim_defaults`.

redshift

: Default is set in `~halotools.sim_manager.sim_defaults`.

mdef: str, optional

: String specifying the halo mass definition, e.g., 'vir' or '200m'.
  Default is set in `~halotools.empirical_models.model_defaults`.

halo_boundary_key

: Default behavior is to use the column associated with the input mdef.

conc_mass_model

: Specifies the function used to model the relation between
  NFW concentration and halo mass.
  Can either be a custom-built callable function,
  or one of the following strings:
  `dutton_maccio14`, `direct_from_halo_catalog`.

concentration_key

: Column name of the halo catalog storing NFW concentration.

  This argument is only relevant when `conc_mass_model`
  is set to `direct_from_halo_catalog`. In such a case,
  the default value is `halo_nfw_conc`,
  which is consistent with all halo catalogs provided by Halotools
  but may differ from the convention adopted in custom halo catalogs.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> nfw = NFWProfile()
```

methods.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.nfw_profile.NFWProfile.conc_NFWmodel
documentation:
NFW concentration as a function of halo mass.

# Parameters

prim_haloprop

: Array storing the mass-like variable, e.g., `halo_mvir`.

  If `prim_haloprop` is not passed,
  then `table` keyword argument must be passed.

table

: `~astropy.table.Table` storing the halo catalog.

  If your NFW model is based on the virial definition,
  then `halo_mvir` must appear in the input table,
  and likewise for other halo mass definitions.

  If `table` is not passed,
  then `prim_haloprop` keyword argument must be passed.

# Returns

conc

: Concentrations of the input halos.

  Note that concentrations will be clipped to their min/max permitted
  values set in the `~halotools.empirical_models.model_defaults` module.
  The purpose of this clipping is to ensure stable results during
  mock galaxy population. Due to this clipping,
  the behavior of the `conc_NFWmodel` function
  is different from the concentration-mass relation that underlies it.

# Examples

In the examples below, we'll demonstrate the various ways to use the
`~halotools.empirical_models.NFWProfile.conc_NFWmodel` function, depending
on the initial choice for the `conc_mass_model`.

```pycon
>>> fake_masses = np.logspace(12, 15, 10)
```

If you use the `direct_from_halo_catalog` option, you must pass a
`table` argument storing a `~astropy.table.Table` with a column name
for the halo mass that is consistent with your chosen halo mass definition:

```pycon
>>> from astropy.table import Table
>>> nfw = NFWProfile(conc_mass_model='direct_from_halo_catalog', mdef='vir')
>>> fake_conc = np.zeros_like(fake_masses) + 5.
>>> fake_halo_table = Table({'halo_mvir': fake_masses, 'halo_nfw_conc': fake_conc})
>>> model_conc = nfw.conc_NFWmodel(table=fake_halo_table)
```

In case your halo catalog uses a different keyname from the Halotools
default `halo_nfw_conc`:

```pycon
>>> nfw = NFWProfile(conc_mass_model='direct_from_halo_catalog', mdef='vir', concentration_key='my_conc_keyname')
>>> fake_halo_table = Table({'halo_mvir': fake_masses, 'my_conc_keyname': fake_conc})
>>> model_conc = nfw.conc_NFWmodel(table=fake_halo_table)
```

One of the available options provided by Halotools is `dutton_maccio14`.
With this option, you can either pass in a `table` argument, or alternatively
an array of masses via the `prim_haloprop` argument:

```pycon
>>> nfw = NFWProfile(conc_mass_model='dutton_maccio14')
>>> fake_halo_table = Table({'halo_mvir': fake_masses, 'halo_nfw_conc': fake_conc})
>>> model_conc = nfw.conc_NFWmodel(table=fake_halo_table)
>>> model_conc = nfw.conc_NFWmodel(prim_haloprop=fake_masses)
```

Finally, you may also have chosen to define your own concentration-mass relation.
If so, your function must at a minimum accept a `table` keyword argument.
Below we give a trivial example of using the identity function:

```pycon
>>> def identity_func(*args, **kwargs): return kwargs['table']['halo_mvir']
>>> nfw = NFWProfile(conc_mass_model=identity_func, mdef='vir')
>>> fake_halo_table = Table({'halo_mvir': fake_masses})
>>> model_conc = nfw.conc_NFWmodel(table=fake_halo_table)
```

methods.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.nfw_profile.NFWProfile.dimensionless_mass_density
documentation:
Physical density of the NFW halo scaled by the density threshold of the mass definition.

The `dimensionless_mass_density` is defined as
$\tilde{\rho}_{\rm prof}(\tilde{r}) \equiv \rho_{\rm prof}(\tilde{r}) / \rho_{\rm thresh}$,
where $\tilde{r}\equiv r/R_{\Delta}$.

For an NFW halo,
$\tilde{\rho}_{\rm NFW}(\tilde{r}, c) = \frac{c^{3}/3g(c)}{c\tilde{r}(1 + c\tilde{r})^{2}},$

where $g(x) \equiv \log(1+x) - x / (1+x)$ is computed using the `g` function.

The quantity $\rho_{\rm thresh}$ is a function of
the halo mass definition, cosmology and redshift,
and is computed via the
`~halotools.empirical_models.profile_helpers.density_threshold` function.
The quantity $\rho_{\rm prof}$ is the physical mass density of the
halo profile and is computed via the `mass_density` function.
See {ref}`nfw_spatial_profile_derivations` for a derivation of this expression.

# Parameters

scaled_radius

: Halo-centric distance *r* scaled by the halo boundary $R_{\Delta}$, so that
  $0 <= \tilde{r} \equiv r/R_{\Delta} <= 1$. Can be a scalar or numpy array.

conc

: Value of the halo concentration. Can either be a scalar, or a numpy array
  of the same dimension as the input `scaled_radius`.

# Returns

dimensionless_density: array_like

: Dimensionless density of a dark matter halo
  at the input `scaled_radius`, normalized by the
  `~halotools.empirical_models.profile_helpers.density_threshold`
  $\rho_{\rm thresh}$ for the
  halo mass definition, cosmology, and redshift.
  Result is an array of the dimension as the input `scaled_radius`.

methods.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.nfw_profile.NFWProfile.mass_density
documentation:
Physical density of the halo at the input radius,
given in units of $h^{3}/{\rm Mpc}^{3}$.

# Parameters

radius

: Halo-centric distance in Mpc/h units; can be a scalar or numpy array

mass

: Total mass of the halo; can be a scalar or numpy array of the same
  dimension as the input `radius`.

conc

: Value of the halo concentration. Can either be a scalar, or a numpy array
  of the same dimension as the input `radius`.

# Returns

density: array_like

: Physical density of a dark matter halo of the input `mass`
  at the input `radius`. Result is an array of the
  dimension as the input `radius`, reported in units of $h^{3}/Mpc^{3}$.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> model = NFWProfile()
>>> Npts = 100
>>> radius = np.logspace(-2, -1, Npts)
>>> mass = np.zeros(Npts) + 1e12
>>> conc = 5
>>> result = model.mass_density(radius, mass, conc)
>>> concarr = np.linspace(1, 100, Npts)
>>> result = model.mass_density(radius, mass, concarr)
```

# Notes

See {ref}`halo_profile_definitions` for derivations and implementation details.

methods.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.nfw_profile.NFWProfile.cumulative_mass_PDF
documentation:
Analytical result for the fraction of the total mass
enclosed within dimensionless radius of an NFW halo,

$P_{\rm NFW}(<\tilde{r}) \equiv M_{\Delta}(<\tilde{r}) / M_{\Delta} = g(c\tilde{r})/g(\tilde{r}),$

where $g(x) \equiv \int_{0}^{x}dy\frac{y}{(1+y)^{2}} = \log(1+x) - x / (1+x)$ is computed
using `g`, and where $\tilde{r} \equiv r / R_{\Delta}$.

See {ref}`nfw_cumulative_mass_pdf_derivation` for a derivation of this expression.

# Parameters

scaled_radius

: Halo-centric distance *r* scaled by the halo boundary $R_{\Delta}$, so that
  $0 <= \tilde{r} \equiv r/R_{\Delta} <= 1$. Can be a scalar or numpy array.

conc

: Value of the halo concentration. Can either be a scalar, or a numpy array
  of the same dimension as the input `scaled_radius`.

# Returns

p: array_like

: The fraction of the total mass enclosed
  within the input `scaled_radius`, in $M_{\odot}/h$;
  has the same dimensions as the input `scaled_radius`.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> model = NFWProfile()
>>> Npts = 100
>>> scaled_radius = np.logspace(-2, 0, Npts)
>>> conc = 5
>>> result = model.cumulative_mass_PDF(scaled_radius, conc)
>>> concarr = np.linspace(1, 100, Npts)
>>> result = model.cumulative_mass_PDF(scaled_radius, concarr)
```

methods.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.nfw_profile.NFWProfile.enclosed_mass
documentation:
The mass enclosed within the input radius,
$M(<r) = 4\pi\int_{0}^{r}dr'r'^{2}\rho(r)$.

# Parameters

radius

: Halo-centric distance in Mpc/h units; can be a scalar or numpy array

total_mass

: Total mass of the halo; can be a scalar or numpy array of the same
  dimension as the input `radius`.

: Value of the halo concentration. Can either be a scalar, or a numpy array
  of the same dimension as the input `radius`.

# Returns

enclosed_mass: array_like

: The mass enclosed within radius r, in $M_{\odot}/h$;
  has the same dimensions as the input `radius`.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> model = NFWProfile()
>>> Npts = 100
>>> radius = np.logspace(-2, -1, Npts)
>>> total_mass = np.zeros(Npts) + 1e12
>>> conc = 5
>>> result = model.enclosed_mass(radius, total_mass, conc)
>>> concarr = np.linspace(1, 100, Npts)
```

methods.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.nfw_profile.NFWProfile.virial_velocity
documentation:
The circular velocity evaluated at the halo boundary,
$V_{\rm vir} \equiv \sqrt{GM_{\rm halo}/R_{\rm halo}}$.

# Parameters

total_mass

: Total mass of the halo; can be a scalar or numpy array.

# Returns

vvir

: Virial velocity in km/s.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> model = NFWProfile()
>>> Npts = 100
>>> mass_array = np.logspace(11, 15, Npts)
>>> vvir_array = model.virial_velocity(mass_array)
```

# Notes

See {ref}`halo_profile_definitions` for derivations and implementation details.

methods.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.nfw_profile.NFWProfile.circular_velocity
documentation:
The circular velocity, $V_{\rm cir} \equiv \sqrt{GM(<r)/r}$,
as a function of halo-centric distance r.

# Parameters

radius

: Halo-centric distance in Mpc/h units; can be a scalar or numpy array

total_mass

: Total mass of the halo; can be a scalar or numpy array of the same
  dimension as the input `radius`.

conc

: Value of the halo concentration. Can either be a scalar, or a numpy array
  of the same dimension as the input `radius`.

# Returns

vc: array_like

: The circular velocity in km/s; has the same dimensions as the input `radius`.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> model = NFWProfile()
>>> Npts = 100
>>> radius = np.logspace(-2, -1, Npts)
>>> total_mass = np.zeros(Npts) + 1e12
>>> conc = 5
>>> result = model.circular_velocity(radius, total_mass, conc)
>>> concarr = np.linspace(1, 100, Npts)
>>> result = model.circular_velocity(radius, total_mass, concarr)
```

# Notes

See {ref}`halo_profile_definitions` for derivations and implementation details.

methods.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.nfw_profile.NFWProfile.rmax
documentation:
Radius at which the halo attains its maximum circular velocity,
$R_{\rm max}^{\rm NFW} = 2.16258R_{\Delta}/c$.

# Parameters

total_mass: array_like

: Total halo mass in $M_{\odot}/h$; can be a number or a numpy array.

conc

: Value of the halo concentration. Can either be a scalar, or a numpy array
  of the same dimension as the input `total_mass`.

# Returns

rmax

: $R_{\rm max}$ in Mpc/h.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> model = NFWProfile()
>>> Npts = 100
>>> total_mass = np.zeros(Npts) + 1e12
>>> conc = 5
>>> result = model.rmax(total_mass, conc)
>>> concarr = np.linspace(1, 100, Npts)
>>> result = model.rmax(total_mass, concarr)
```

# Notes

See {ref}`halo_profile_definitions` for derivations and implementation details.

methods.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.nfw_profile.NFWProfile.vmax

# Parameters

total_mass: array_like

: Total halo mass in $M_{\odot}/h$; can be a number or a numpy array.

conc

: Value of the halo concentration. Can either be a scalar, or a numpy array
  of the same dimension as the input `total_mass`.

# Returns

vmax

: $V_{\rm max}$ in km/s.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> model = NFWProfile()
>>> Npts = 100
>>> total_mass = np.zeros(Npts) + 1e12
>>> conc = 5
>>> result = model.vmax(total_mass, conc)
>>> concarr = np.linspace(1, 100, Npts)
>>> result = model.vmax(total_mass, concarr)
```

See {ref}`halo_profile_definitions` for derivations and implementation details.

  Will have the same dimension as the input `total_mass`.

methods.name: halotools.empirical_models.phase_space_models.analytic_models.satellites.nfw.nfw_profile.NFWProfile.mc_generate_nfw_radial_positions
documentation:
Return a Monte Carlo realization of points in an NFW profile.

See {ref}`monte_carlo_nfw_spatial_profile` for a discussion of this technique.

# Parameters

num_pts

: Number of points in the Monte Carlo realization of the profile.
  Default is 1e4.

conc

: Concentration of the NFW profile being realized.
  Default is 5.

halo_mass

: Total mass of the halo whose profile is being realized,
  used to define the halo boundary for the mass definition
  bound to the NFWProfile instance as `mdef`.

  If `halo_mass` is unspecified,
  keyword argument `halo_radius` must be specified.

halo_radius

: Physical boundary of the halo whose profile is being realized
  in units of Mpc/h.

  If `halo_radius` is unspecified,
  keyword argument `halo_mass` must be specified, in which case the
  outer boundary of the halo will be determined according to the mass definition
  bound to the NFWProfile instance as `mdef`.

seed

: Random number seed used in the Monte Carlo realization.
  Default is None, which will produce stochastic results.

# Returns

radial_positions

: Numpy array storing a Monte Carlo realization of the halo profile.
  All values will lie strictly between 0 and the halo boundary.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> nfw = NFWProfile()
>>> radial_positions = nfw.mc_generate_nfw_radial_positions(halo_mass = 1e12, conc = 10)
>>> radial_positions = nfw.mc_generate_nfw_radial_positions(halo_radius = 0.25)
```

Model for the spatial distribution of mass
and/or galaxies residing in an NFW halo profile,
based on Navarro, Frenk and White (1995),
[arXiv:9508025](http://arxiv.org/abs/astro-ph/9508025/).

For a review of the mathematics underlying the NFW profile,
including descriptions of how the relevant equations are
implemented in the Halotools code base, see {ref}`nfw_profile_tutorial`.
